SateNet
UX Research Report · April 2026
Research report

Internet purchase
usability study

Moderated in-person sessions testing M-Pesa and Fedi payment flows for SataNet internet bundles. Nairobi community, April 2026.

4 tasks SUS · SEQ · Lostness Fedi familiarity segmentation
Flow verdicts: per criteria agreed with Head of Product
Launch-ready
M-Pesa flow
80% unassisted completion · SUS avg 85 · No P0 issues. Meets all pre-agreed thresholds.
Redesign needed
Fedi flow
50% overall · 0% Fedi-unfamiliar · Ecash terminology confusion at critical payment step.

Barrier: no Fedi account, not price
Emotional arc by flow
M-Pesa: smooth throughout
Entry → Package → Pay → Confirm → Connected
Confident throughout. SEQ avg 5.5. M-Pesa is the trusted baseline in this community; everything else is compared against it.
Fedi: dip at ecash terminology
Entry → Tap Fedi → Ecash claim → Confirm → Connected
Confidence dips at ecash. "What is ecash?" is the most common utterance. Recovers for familiar users. Fails entirely for unfamiliar (code 0).
Anchor quotes
"Wow! 40% discount, I am definitely choosing that."
Participant · Task 3 · Chose Fedi delight
"I haven't looked at the packages, but I can see Fedi has a 40% discount, meaning it is cheaper."
Participant · Task 2 · Fedi flow mental model
"What is ecash?"
Participant · Task 2 · Ecash screen confusion
"I would choose Mpesa because I do not have a fedi account."
Participant · Task 3 · Chose M-Pesa mental model
"This is a good way to introduce those who are still not fully into bitcoin."
Participant · M-Pesa flow observation positive observation
Risks to flag
Ecash is unfamiliar and blocks code-0 users entirely and confuses code-1
Fedi completion for code-0 participants is 0%. The ecash terminology and "Claim ecash" label has no context for users who have never heard of ecash. This is a content and onboarding problem, not a UI problem. An inline explanation resolves it but must be tested before build.
Price advantage is visible and motivating, but only works if users have a Fedi account
100% of participants noticed the 40% discount. All who chose M-Pesa did so because they lacked a Fedi account. Fedi account onboarding must be part of the launch plan.

Average SUS 85, above the industry benchmark of 68. P1 (67.5) is the only below-average score, corresponding to the Fedi-unfamiliar (code-0) participant who struggled most with ecash terminology.
Launch readiness
M-Pesa flow: launch-ready
80% unassisted completion meets the pre-agreed threshold. SUS average of 85 exceeds the 68 minimum. No P0 issues. Three P1 issues identified, recommended to fix before release but do not block launch.
Fedi flow: redesign needed before build
0% completion for Fedi-unfamiliar participants triggers the redesign verdict. The ecash claim screen is the primary failure point. Recommended: add ecash explainer at handoff, replace "Claim ecash" with plain-language copy, retest with code-0 participants before build.

Severity matrix: observed behaviour per issue
P1
Fedi: package navigation labels not active paths
"I woulld click choose pacage." (5 of 6 participants)
Fix: Make all package options navigable. Score: 15
Flow: Fedi · Type: nav · Freq: 5/6
P1
Fedi: 40% discount visible but context missing
"I haven't looked at the packages, but I can see Fedi has a 40% discount." (4 of 6)
Fix: Add discount context label on choice screen. Score: 12
Flow: Fedi · Type: nav · Freq: 4/6
P1
Fedi: "Get online" label confusing at start of flow
"Get online is confusing, I usually see this after choosing a bundle and payment." (4 of 6)
Fix: Move "Get online" to post-payment confirmation. Score: 12
Flow: Fedi · Type: nav · Freq: 4/6
P1
Choice: numeric values "20" and "5" without units
"I don't know what 20 and 5 mean." (3 of 6)
Fix: Add currency units to all numeric values on choice screen. Score: 9
Flow: Choice · Type: affordance · Freq: 3/6
P2
Fedi: "Claim ecash" label causes confusion
"Claim ecash, I am confused." (3 of 6)
Fix: Replace with plain language. Add ecash explainer on first encounter. Score: 6
Flow: Fedi · Type: copy · Freq: 3/6
P3
Fedi: "What is ecash?" terminology unfamiliar
"What is ecash?" (2 of 6)
Fix: Inline tooltip on first ecash appearance. Score: 2
Flow: Fedi · Type: handoff · Freq: 2/6
P3
Choice: buttons visually similar to package cards
"The buttons look like the packages, it can be a bit hard to see." (1 of 6)
Fix: Differentiate interactive vs informational elements. Score: 1
Flow: Choice · Type: nav · Freq: 1/6

78% accepted the handoff. The primary friction is not the transition itself but the ecash terminology immediately after it. Fix the copy, not the architecture.
Mental model gaps
Ecash = unfamiliar concept for non-bitcoin users
ExpectedFamiliar terms: M-Pesa, shillings, or sats
Actual"Ecash" and "Claim ecash" appear with no explanation at the critical payment step
"What is ecash?" (2 of 6) participants, heard at the handoff screen
Numeric values require currency context
ExpectedValues shown as "Ksh 20" or similar with unit context
Actual"20" and "5" shown without sufficient unit labels on the choice screen
"I don't know what 20 and 5 mean." (Participant · Choice screen
Package items: all expected to be tappable
ExpectedAll visible package cards are interactive and lead to purchase
ActualNot all items are active paths; 5 of 6 tapped non-active areas on first attempt
"I woulld click choose pacage." Most common first action in Fedi flow

Code-2 (familiar) users chose Fedi 100% of the time. Code-0 chose Fedi 0% and had 0% unassisted completion. The barrier is familiarity and account access, not price, not flow design.
Delight moments: protect in the build
"Wow! 40% discount, I am definitely choosing that."
Spontaneous price reaction. The choice screen price comparison is working. Do not change this layout.
Protect this
"This is a good way to introduce those who are still not fully into bitcoin."
Participant recognised the dual-payment model as an inclusive onramp. The side-by-side presentation is seen as welcoming.
Protect this
"Make the Mpesa column green, so it is easier to tell the difference with Fedi."
Unprompted design suggestion: green for M-Pesa aligns with M-Pesa's brand colour. Low-cost visual differentiation improvement.
Consider for v3

issues
Navigation issues account for 57% of all problems. Primary fixes: making package options navigable and improving label copy: these are design changes with no engineering dependency. Start there.
Liaison cultural field notes
M-Pesa as the trust baseline
Every participant used M-Pesa as their reference point for payment expectations. Speed, confirmation patterns, and terminology were all compared against M-Pesa. The SataNet M-Pesa flow performs well against this baseline; participants found it familiar and predictable.
Fedi and ecash: unfamiliar but not hostile
Ecash confusion was practical, not fearful. Participants who asked "What is ecash?" were curious, not worried. A simple one-sentence explanation at the right moment resolves this. The 40% price saving created genuine motivation to use Fedi. The barrier is knowledge, not distrust.
Participant-volunteered design suggestions
Two participants suggested improvements unprompted: green for the M-Pesa column (aligning with M-Pesa brand recognition) and making all package cards tappable. When users volunteer design directions without prompting, the pain is real and the solution is obvious to them.

Affinity map synthesised from participant sessions across 4 tasks. Each card is a direct observation or verbatim quote. Clusters represent emergent themes, not pre-imposed categories.

Theme cluster: price awareness
Price is visible. Adoption barrier is not price.
"I can see Fedi has a 40% discount, meaning it is cheaper."P6 · T2 · noticed
"I see Fedi is much cheaper, only 8 bob compared to 20 bob."P2 · T3 · noticed
100% of participants noticed the price differencemetric · T3
"I don't know what 20 and 5 mean."P4 · T3 · no units
"I would choose Mpesa because I do not have a fedi account."P1 · T3 · barrier
Only 2 of 6 chose M-Pesa, both citing lack of accountmetric · T3
Insight: The 40% saving is universally noticed. The barrier to adoption is account access and ecash familiarity, not price visibility.
Theme cluster: ecash & handoff confusion
Ecash is unfamiliar. The handoff itself is accepted.
"What is ecash?" (2 of 6) participantsP1, P2 · T2 · critical
"Claim ecash, I am confused." (3 of 6)multiple · T2 · copy issue
Handoff reaction: Confused 1, Worried 1metric · T2
Handoff reaction: Accepted 7 of 9metric · T2
Code-2 familiar users: 100% Fedi completionmetric · T2 · seg
Code-0 unfamiliar users: 0% Fedi completionmetric · T2 · seg
Insight: The transition to the Fedi app is broadly accepted (78%). What blocks users is the ecash terminology after the handoff. Fix the copy, not the architecture.
Theme cluster: navigation and affordance
Package navigation is the #1 friction point by frequency.
"I woulld click choose pacage." (5 of 6)multiple · T1/T2 · P1 issue
"The buttons look like the packages, hard to see."P4 · T3 · affordance
"Get online is confusing at the start of the flow."P6 · T2 · label
"I haven't looked at the packages but I see 40% discount." (4 of 6)multiple · T2
Fix: Make all package options navigable active pathsPriority score: 15
Insight: Nav issues account for 57% of all logged problems. These are design changes with no engineering dependency; fastest path to improved completion rates.
Theme cluster: delight moments (protect in build)
Three things participants loved unprompted.
"Wow! 40% discount, I am definitely choosing that."P3 · T3 · price visibility
"This is a good way to introduce those not fully into bitcoin."P2 · T1 · dual payment
"Make the Mpesa column green, easier to tell the difference."P3 · T1 · design suggestion
Insight: The side-by-side payment model is perceived as inclusive and welcoming, not confusing. The price comparison layout works. Do not redesign these elements.

Interpretation: The flow works for familiar users and fails entirely for unfamiliar ones. Recommendation: user education before launch, not flow redesign.

Colour coding
Green: Pass, positive finding, good metric. Something that works and should be protected.
Amber / orange: Warning, friction point, Fedi-specific data. Needs attention but not a blocker.
Red: Fail, critical issue, redesign needed. Blocks completion or trust.
Teal: Neutral informational metric. No positive or negative judgement implied.
Severity ratings (P0–P3)
P0
Blocker: participant cannot complete the task at all. Must be fixed before any build starts.
P1
Major: significant friction, completes with difficulty or long delay. Fix before build.
P2
Moderate: minor confusion, participant recovers without help. Fix before release.
P3
Minor: cosmetic or edge case only. Improve in next iteration.
Task results (P / A / F / AB)
P
Pass: Participant completed the task correctly without any help. The only result that counts as unassisted completion in the report.
A
Assisted: Participant completed the task but needed a prompt or redirect from the moderator.
F
Fail: Participant attempted the task but did not complete it despite trying.
AB
Abandoned: Participant stopped trying before completing. They chose to stop, distinct from a Fail.
Metrics explained
SUS
System Usability Scale: A 10-question standardised usability score from 0–100. Industry benchmark: below 68 = poor, 68–80 = good, above 80 = excellent. Our average: 85.
SEQ
Single Ease Question: Participant rates task difficulty immediately after completing it. Scale 1 (very difficult) to 7 (very easy). Captured after each task.
NPS
Net Promoter Score: "How likely are you to recommend this app?" Scale 0–10. Promoters: 9–10, Passives: 7–8, Detractors: 0–6. Score = % Promoters − % Detractors.
Lostness
Lostness score: Actual taps ÷ optimal taps. A score of 1.0× means the participant took the perfect path. 2.0× means they took twice as many taps as needed. Lower is better.
Priority score
Issue priority score: Formula: (4 − severity level) × frequency. A P0 issue experienced by 6 participants scores 24. Sorts issues by business impact, not just severity.
Fedi familiarity codes
Code 0
Participant has never heard of Fedi or bitcoin. No prior context for ecash.
Code 1
Participant has heard of Fedi or bitcoin but has not used it.
Code 2
Participant has used Fedi before. Has an existing wallet and familiarity with ecash.
Study overview
Method
Moderated in-person usability sessions. Researcher observes participant completing tasks on a Figma prototype while thinking aloud.
Participants
Community participants recruited in Nairobi. Mix of Fedi familiarity codes 0, 1, and 2. All regular mobile data users with M-Pesa accounts.
Tasks
T1: Buy internet via M-Pesa (free choice). T2: Buy internet via Fedi flow. T3: Free choice after experiencing both. T4: Verbal comprehension probe: price awareness check.
Date
April 2026, Nairobi.

SateNet x Fedi UX Research Report · Nairobi, April 2026
